answers:
**1. Root cause of the cross-tenant leak.**
File: `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`), consumed by `cedarline/backend/app/db.py:6` (`create_engine(os.environ["DATABASE_URL"])`).

`DATABASE_URL` is set to `postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar` — the schema-owning Postgres role that ran the migrations (`cedarline/scripts/migrate.sh`), not the dedicated `cedar_app` role from `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`. In Postgres, a table owner (and any role with `BYPASSRLS`, which table owners effectively have with respect to their own tables) is exempt from row-level security policies by default, regardless of any `SET app.current_tenant = ...` executed per-request in `deps.py`. The `tenant_isolation` RLS policies defined in `cedarline/db/migrations/0001_init.sql` on `clients` and `appointments` are real and correctly written (`tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant')::uuid`), but since the app connects as the owning role, Postgres silently skips evaluating that policy — no error, no warning. `GET /clients` therefore returns every tenant's rows, not just the requesting tenant's, exactly matching the reported symptom. This is precisely the "two-role model" hazard called out in the environment-and-build-hazards skill: whenever the app connects with the migration/owner role "because it was easier" (or, here, because nobody flipped `.env` after bootstrapping), every RLS isolation guarantee becomes silently inert.

**2. Correct fix.**
Change `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`, and any deployed equivalent) so the running application's `DATABASE_URL` uses the least-privilege `cedar_app` role/password (`postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`), not `cedar_owner`. Structurally, going forward the app should enforce two clearly separated roles as a standing convention, matching what the test suite (`tests/conftest.py`) already assumes:
- `cedar_owner`: used only by `scripts/migrate.sh` / migration tooling, never referenced by the running backend.
- `cedar_app`: the only role the FastAPI app (`app/db.py`, `app/deps.py`) ever connects with; it has `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` (already granted) but no bypass/owner/superuser privileges, so RLS policies are actually enforced on every query.
It would also be worth adding an automated check (e.g., a startup assertion or the same kind of test as `test_isolation.py`'s `assert "cedar_app" in TEST_DATABASE_URL`, but applied to the *app's own* `DATABASE_URL`) so a misconfigured `.env` fails loudly instead of silently leaking data — since Postgres gives no runtime signal when RLS is bypassed this way.

**3. Why the app role disappears after a local DB wipe, and what's needed to fix it.**
`cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v` (which deletes the `cedar_pgdata` named volume — the entire Postgres data directory, including all roles) then `docker compose up -d db` (a fresh Postgres cluster with only the roles the image's own init env vars create, i.e. `cedar_owner`) and finally only `./scripts/migrate.sh`. It never re-runs `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`. That bootstrap file's own header states it is "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup," and `docs/local-setup.md` confirms creating `cedar_app` is a separate manual step (step 2) done only once, while "Reset your local db" (the reset script) documents only re-running migrations. So after any `-v` wipe/rebuild, the `cedar_app` role genuinely no longer exists in the new cluster, and anything connecting as `cedar_app` (notably the isolation test suite's default `TEST_DATABASE_URL` in `tests/conftest.py`) fails immediately with a "role does not exist" error. To fix it, someone must manually re-run `psql "postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` after every reset — best done by adding that step to `reset-local-db.sh` itself so it isn't forgotten.
report:
I have everything needed. Writing the report.

## Plan
Investigate a reported cross-tenant data leak on `GET /clients` and a broken app-role connection after a local DB rebuild, in a repo containing many sample projects. Scoped to `cedarline/` since it's the only project with `db/bootstrap`, RLS-style migrations, and a `reset-local-db.sh` script matching the two symptoms. Read the environment-and-build-hazards skill first (it explicitly describes this exact "two-role model" hazard), then inspected docker-compose, migrations, bootstrap SQL, env config, docs, and backend app code.

## Actions
- Read `.claude/skills/environment-and-build-hazards/SKILL.md` — it describes exactly this hazard: RLS-enforcing databases need a schema-owning role (migrations only) and a separate least-privilege app role; connecting with the owner role silently bypasses RLS with no error, and the app role is often created by a one-time bootstrap script that must be re-run after any data-volume wipe.
- Ran `find`/`ls` across the repo root — found 15+ sibling sample projects; only `cedarline/` has `db/bootstrap/`, `db/migrations/`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `scripts/reset-local-db.sh`, matching the reported symptoms.
- Read `cedarline/docker-compose.yml` — Postgres service created with superuser role `cedar_owner`/`ownerpw`, DB `cedar`. No bootstrap SQL is mounted or run automatically.
- Read `cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh` — runs `docker compose down -v` (destroys the data volume), brings the DB back up, then only runs `./scripts/migrate.sh`. It never re-runs `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`.
- Read `cedarline/scripts/migrate.sh` — connects and runs migrations as `cedar_owner` (correct for migrations).
- Read `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` — creates role `cedar_app LOGIN PASSWORD 'apppw'`, grants it `SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE` on all tables. Header comment: "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup."
- Read `cedarline/db/migrations/0001_init.sql` — creates `tenants`, `clients`, `appointments`; enables `ROW LEVEL SECURITY` and a `tenant_isolation` policy on `clients` and `appointments`, filtering on `tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant')::uuid`.
- Read `cedarline/.env` and `cedarline/.env.example` — both set `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`, i.e. the app's own runtime connection string uses the schema-owning role, not `cedar_app`.
- Read `cedarline/docs/local-setup.md` — confirms first-time setup requires manually running the bootstrap SQL as a separate, one-time step, and that "reset your local db" only re-runs migrations.
- Read `cedarline/backend/app/db.py` — `engine = create_engine(os.environ["DATABASE_URL"])`, i.e. the running application connects using whatever `DATABASE_URL` is configured (currently `cedar_owner`).
- Read `cedarline/backend/app/deps.py` — `tenant_connection()` opens a connection from that engine and does `SET app.current_tenant = :tenant_id` per request, relying entirely on the RLS policy to actually restrict rows.
- Read `cedarline/backend/tests/conftest.py` and `test_isolation.py` — the isolation test suite explicitly connects via `TEST_DATABASE_URL`, defaulting to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`, and asserts `"cedar_app" in TEST_DATABASE_URL`.

## Answers

**1. Root cause of the cross-tenant leak.**
File: `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`), consumed by `cedarline/backend/app/db.py:6` (`create_engine(os.environ["DATABASE_URL"])`).

`DATABASE_URL` is set to `postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar` — the schema-owning Postgres role that ran the migrations (`cedarline/scripts/migrate.sh`), not the dedicated `cedar_app` role from `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`. In Postgres, a table owner (and any role with `BYPASSRLS`, which table owners effectively have with respect to their own tables) is exempt from row-level security policies by default, regardless of any `SET app.current_tenant = ...` executed per-request in `deps.py`. The `tenant_isolation` RLS policies defined in `cedarline/db/migrations/0001_init.sql` on `clients` and `appointments` are real and correctly written (`tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant')::uuid`), but since the app connects as the owning role, Postgres silently skips evaluating that policy — no error, no warning. `GET /clients` therefore returns every tenant's rows, not just the requesting tenant's, exactly matching the reported symptom. This is precisely the "two-role model" hazard called out in the environment-and-build-hazards skill: whenever the app connects with the migration/owner role "because it was easier" (or, here, because nobody flipped `.env` after bootstrapping), every RLS isolation guarantee becomes silently inert.

**2. Correct fix.**
Change `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`, and any deployed equivalent) so the running application's `DATABASE_URL` uses the least-privilege `cedar_app` role/password (`postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`), not `cedar_owner`. Structurally, going forward the app should enforce two clearly separated roles as a standing convention, matching what the test suite (`tests/conftest.py`) already assumes:
- `cedar_owner`: used only by `scripts/migrate.sh` / migration tooling, never referenced by the running backend.
- `cedar_app`: the only role the FastAPI app (`app/db.py`, `app/deps.py`) ever connects with; it has `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` (already granted) but no bypass/owner/superuser privileges, so RLS policies are actually enforced on every query.
It would also be worth adding an automated check (e.g., a startup assertion or the same kind of test as `test_isolation.py`'s `assert "cedar_app" in TEST_DATABASE_URL`, but applied to the *app's own* `DATABASE_URL`) so a misconfigured `.env` fails loudly instead of silently leaking data — since Postgres gives no runtime signal when RLS is bypassed this way.

**3. Why the app role disappears after a local DB wipe, and what's needed to fix it.**
`cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v` (which deletes the `cedar_pgdata` named volume — the entire Postgres data directory, including all roles) then `docker compose up -d db` (a fresh Postgres cluster with only the roles the image's own init env vars create, i.e. `cedar_owner`) and finally only `./scripts/migrate.sh`. It never re-runs `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`. That bootstrap file's own header states it is "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup," and `docs/local-setup.md` confirms creating `cedar_app` is a separate manual step (step 2) done only once, while "Reset your local db" (the reset script) documents only re-running migrations. So after any `-v` wipe/rebuild, the `cedar_app` role genuinely no longer exists in the new cluster, and anything connecting as `cedar_app` (notably the isolation test suite's default `TEST_DATABASE_URL` in `tests/conftest.py`) fails immediately with a "role does not exist" error. To fix it, someone must manually re-run `psql "postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` after every reset — best done by adding that step to `reset-local-db.sh` itself so it isn't forgotten.

## Next steps
If allowed to make changes, I would: (a) update `cedarline/.env`/`.env.example` to use `cedar_app` for the application's `DATABASE_URL`, keeping `cedar_owner` only in `migrate.sh`; (b) add `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` execution to `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` (idempotently, e.g. `CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS`/`DO $$...$$` guard) so a wipe-and-rebuild always leaves a working `cedar_app` role; (c) add a lightweight automated guard (unit test or startup check) asserting the app's own runtime `DATABASE_URL` is never the owner/superuser role, so this class of bug fails CI instead of leaking data silently; (d) update `docs/local-setup.md` to reflect that bootstrap is no longer a manual one-time step, if (b) is done.

## Confidence and gaps
High confidence on all three answers — each is backed by direct file contents (`.env`, `docker-compose.yml`, migration SQL, bootstrap SQL, `db.py`, `deps.py`, `reset-local-db.sh`, `migrate.sh`, `local-setup.md`, and the test suite's own assumptions in `conftest.py`/`test_isolation.py`), not inference from an unread file. I did not execute anything (per read-only constraint), so I have not empirically reproduced the leak or the role-missing error by actually running the stack/tests — the conclusions rest on static analysis of Postgres RLS/role-bypass semantics and the scripts' logic, which is unambiguous here (owner-role bypass of RLS is documented core Postgres behavior, and the missing bootstrap step in the reset script is directly visible in the script text). One minor gap: I did not check whether any other config source (e.g., CI secrets, a shell profile, or Docker env override) might override `.env`'s `DATABASE_URL` at runtime — within the repo as inspected, `.env`/`.env.example` are the only sources referenced by `app/db.py`.
